item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It appears one of the previous cycles got a bit too far…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. It walks the line between plausible and bizarre I enjoy, in both the technical writing (the technobabble is dry and believable enough to think a machine like this might exist, but it's confusing enough to be unsettling) and concept (a machine to trap hostile time travellers is both simple/logical, at least in the Foundation universe, but ridiculous at the same time). The nod to this in the matter-of-fact and "what the fuck" O5 notes is a nice touch. This deserves more attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The world is the world is, love and life ARE DEEP. Maybe as his skies, are, wiiiiiiiiiiddddddddeeeeeeeeee.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To clarify: The Second Law of Thermodynamics is hypothesized, in that someone made a hypothesis regarding it in the past, but is not hypothetical, in that it has been proven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think in the line

hypothesized relation to the gravitational anomaly.

you want

hypothetical relation to the gravitational anomaly.

Hypothesized is more properly used to describe the one doing the hypothesizing then the phenomenon the hypothesis regards, unless you mean to make reference to a specific past event in which someone hypothesized about something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A time-traveler flytrap with unknown origin? +1.

I have no complaints regarding the generous amount of exposition and hypothesizing in this SCP - it feels natural considering the scientific attitude the Foundation seems to be taking with this facility. Some articles work perfectly fine without being mysterious, I feel.

I agree that 'million square meters' is very awkward, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Resized the first and second images to more manageable sizes, originally 3.7 MB and 6 MB respectively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems an odd choice to describe the area in millions square meters when it's exactly the same and far more straightforward in square kilometers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wasn't the biggest fan of the execution on this but I love the concept enough to give it an upvote.

Also wow, returning legend. Any chance you'll do some writing for Resurrection at some point in the future? There's been a lot of people missing it lately ever since this animated short about it was released.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the clearest bit of telling being a problem is the description of the red room contents. You give a bunch of phenomena that aren't easy to visualize or coherent, then just tell us what they are… using more terminology and phenomena that are difficult to visualize.

Unless the title is something really obvious (I rarely see titles), the only evidence I can see for this being a time traveler trap (which is a cool idea) is the hand-drawn diagram and the mention of the matter being from the future. I'm very curious about the origin and reason it's abandoned, but the only place I got any inkling was when you just told us what the O5s think.

Overall I guess… the ratio of mundane detail/investigation to story building was weighted heavily toward the former. I could've lived not knowing, but you have way more than what would've been sufficient detail to hook me and confined the reveals to little designated exposition blobs instead of being integrated into the story, so it just feels like too little too late.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it. Little bit telly, but I likely wouldn't have understood the skip otherwise. The idea is super original and the foreshadowing, as well as the ending itself, was very satisfying. This goes in my box of personal favorite skips and I give it 9/10. Take this with a grain of salt, as I haven't written a single skip for the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article suffers from heavy tell-instead-of-show, but the concept is original enough and the technobabble satisfying enough to make the exposition work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Really well done. I love time travel stuff, and temporal stuff skips, and this is a new idea that I hadn't ever heard of or conceived of.

+ Show
- Hide

I think my favorite part about it is the pressure/temperature gradients. Most people don't think about how time dilation would affect normal things like pressure/temperature/gravity/force or they hand-wave it away, and I am glad to see it is central to the skip. Everything here is really well structured.

My -only- complaint would be that there's not quite enough "show, don't tell." You give us a lot of theories and a lot of exposition, when there might be a way to describe it and let us draw our own conclusions and theories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops! Thanks for the catch, I'll fix that in a second here.
Edit: Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's still some SCP-XXXXs in the picture, along with the picture calling the Red Room the Black Room.

Other than that, this was really cool. I personally love the minor format screw.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Catch the mist, catch the myth, catch the mystery, catch the drift.

The idea of the Foundation having a giant butterfly net designed to stop future time travellers from fucking with the past and throwing everything to hell is cool enough. The way the document's structured also adds in narrative pizzazz in a way that doesn't feel contrived, and that's enough to push me into an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First image from the Library of Congress, picture from some kind of radioactive materials research lab (and then cropped), public domain.

Second two images by me, CC BY-SA 3.0.

I think your reading experience will improved by reading the first two addenda (through ARE YOU SURE YOU WISH TO PROCEED), trying to figure out what's going on, and then doing the final two (after PROCEED). But listen, I don't run your life.

(I feel like I should mention that there was a tumblr post going around recently with a similar thing, but all I can say is that I'd had a convergent idea a while back, and it reminded me I'd had this draft sitting in my sandbox for years and I thought it was decent.) This isn't technically a coldpost, but honestly, I last got feedback on it like two years ago and don't actually remember what it was. Spooky!
